title: 3. Catuposathiyajātaka
claim: the nāga-king (with garuḷa/venateyya, gandhabba-king, the Kuru king) declares his virtue is khanti (patience) — nāga as one of four exemplary kings

title: 9. Mahāpadumajātaka
claim: a hooded nāga (nāgo jātaphaṇo), mighty, caught the falling prince in its coils and saved him in a mountain abyss

title: 10. Mahāvāṇijajātaka
claim: nāgas emerge fully armed (sannaddhā paṇḍavīsati) with archers to attack tree-fellers — a nāga host guarding/avenging

title: 10. Campeyyajātaka
claim: Campeyya the nāga-king keeps uposatha as a serpent (uraga), is captured by a snake-charmer, freed, and invites the king to his nāga palace; bodhisatta-as-nāga

title: 8. Paṇḍaranāgarājajātaka
claim: the nāga-king Paṇḍaraka is seized by the supaṇṇa once his secret is betrayed (Nāgaṃ yathā paṇḍarakaṃ supaṇṇo)

title: 4. Saṅkhapālajātaka
claim: Saṅkhapāla the nāga-king keeps uposatha enduring torment by hunters, longing to be reborn human (manussayoniṃ abhipatthayāno); bodhisatta-as-nāga

title: 6. Bhūridattajātaka
claim: Bhūridatta the nāga: a snake-charmer threatens 'alampāno hi nāgena' and boasts of mastering venomless/venomous nāgas; the nāga is the bodhisatta serpent

title: Vidhurajātaka
claim: Vidhura: the nāga realm with nāga-king/queen (nāgarājā, nāgi), nāga-maidens (nāgakaññā), nāga-kingdom (nāgakula); a nāgi practising non-malice attains the deva-world

title: 6. Sīlavīmaṁsakajātaka
claim: a venomous nāga (ghoraviso nāgo), being virtuous, is not killed — virtue protects it

title: Nidhikaṇḍasutta
claim: Nidhikaṇḍa: nāgas can spirit away a buried treasure (Nāgā vā apanāmenti), as yakkhas carry it off

title: Vammikasutta
claim: Vammika riddle: digging the anthill one finds a nāga; 'leave the nāga, do not strike it, pay homage' — the nāga = the arahant (allegory), literal referent a serpent in the mound

title: 2. Sāsanapaṭṭhānadutiyabhūmi
claim: The Buddha tames/teaches devas, nāgas and yakkhas in their own dialects via Dhamma

title: 51. Surāpānasikkhāpada
claim: A nāga in the fire-room smokes and blazes; its fire is mastered only by the fire-element

title: 6. Kuṭikārasikkhāpada
claim: The nāga-king Maṇikaṇṭha coils a sage sevenfold and rears a great hood

title: 1. Mahākhandhaka
claim: A fierce nāga-king of the fire-room is potent, terribly venomous; the Buddha subdues its fire

title: Suddhikasutta
claim: There are four nāga births: egg-born, womb-born, moisture-born, spontaneously-born

title: Catutthasutasutta
claim: Ambivalent (dual) bodily/verbal/mental conduct + craving leads to rebirth among spontaneously-born nāgas

title: Aṇḍajadānūpakārasuttadasaka
claim: Dual conduct plus specific giving leads to rebirth among egg-born nāgas (dīghāyu, vaṇṇavanto)

title: Paṇītatarasutta
claim: Among the four nāga births each later mode is superior to the egg-born

title: Jalābujādidānūpakārasuttattiṁsaka
claim: Dual conduct plus specific giving leads to rebirth among womb/moisture/spontaneously-born nāgas

title: Uposathasutta
claim: Egg-born nāgas keep the uposatha and so release the body, recalling their former dual conduct

title: Dutiyauposathasutta
claim: Womb-born nāgas keep the uposatha and release the body

title: Tatiyauposathasutta
claim: Moisture-born nāgas keep the uposatha and release the body

title: Catutthauposathasutta
claim: Spontaneously-born nāgas keep the uposatha, resolving on good conduct to escape the nāga state

title: Sutasutta
claim: Dual conduct, with hearing that egg-born nāgas are long-lived/handsome, leads to rebirth among them

title: Dutiyasutasutta
claim: Dual conduct leads to rebirth among womb-born nāgas

title: Tatiyasutasutta
claim: Dual conduct leads to rebirth among moisture-born nāgas

title: Harantisutta
claim: Supaṇṇas seize nāgas only of their own or lower birth-class (egg/womb/moisture/spontaneous)

title: Bakabrahmasutta
claim: A cruel nāga in the Ganges seizes a boat, craving human prey; the bodhisatta frees it

title: Ajitattheraapadāna
claim: nāgas and garuḷas come to the Buddha desiring to hear Dhamma

title: Sabbaphaladāyakattheraapadāna
claim: long-lived nāgas, possessed of iddhi and great renown, come under the donor's sway

title: Uppalavaṇṇātherīapadāna
claim: a nāga-king hosts and feeds the Buddha in the nāga realm and a nāga-maiden makes the anumodanā

title: Uposathasutta
claim: the great ocean is the dwelling of mighty beings including nāgas, with bodies up to hundreds of yojanas

title: Kaṅkhāvitaraṇavisuddhiniddesavaṇṇanā
claim: Bhūridatta-type nāgas (and yāpanaka-petas) have karma supporting/oppressing in apāya, so the four kamma-types' kusala/akusala status holds for them

title: Dasaiddhikathāvaṇṇanā
claim: an obstacle (mountain/tree) conjured by nāgas etc. does not block an iddhi-traveler's passage

title: Nandopanandanāgadamanakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: nāgas (with garuḷas, kumbhaṇḍas, yakkhas) occupy and guard the paribhaṇḍa terraces of Sineru

title: Nandopanandanāgadamanakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: the nāga (Nandopananda) abandons his nāga-form, opening his mouth to swallow the monk

title: Nandopanandanāgadamanakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: by transformation a being can display nāga-form, elephant-form, etc.; nāga-appearance among shapeshift options

title: Nandopanandanāgadamanakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: the method of creating forms applies equally to nāga-forms etc. (refuting that nāga-creation does not fit)

title: Paccayapariggahakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: for nāgas (with Devadatta and yāpanaka-petas) akusala-vipāka-supporting kamma operates in the lower realms, so the kamma-types' status holds

title: Manomayiddhiñāṇakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: by nāga-iddhi one stays in one's own (serpent) species, not becoming a different-species form

title: Ussādanāvaṇṇanā
claim: the wind from a supaṇṇa beating its wings when seizing a nāga (nāga-gahaṇa); supaṇṇas prey on nāgas

title: Pāṭaliputtanagaramāpanavaṇṇanā
claim: nāgaggāha = the apportioning/seizing of nāgas' dwelling-places (a vatthuvijjā category)

title: Devasabhāvaṇṇanā
claim: a nāga-king is the chief of nāgas but is not himself of nāga-birth

title: Devatāsannipātavaṇṇanā
claim: a nāga-king and his large lineage-retinue have long dwelt at Nāgasadaha (the Tacchaka nāga assembly)

title: Devatāsannipātavaṇṇanā
claim: the aforesaid nāgas, overcome by greed for food (āhāralobha), are seized [by supaṇṇas]

title: Candimasūriyādipātubhāvavaṇṇanā
claim: a celestial 'path' (vīthi) is called nāgavīthī because, being watery, it suits nāgas

title: Satisampajaññakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: a nāga-anthill (nāgavammika) is the dwelling of nāgas; used as image of the self-empty body

title: Manomayiddhiñāṇakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: this is said of mighty nāgas: by nāga-iddhi one stays in one's own serpent-species (at rebirth, death, mating, shedding skin), not a different-species form

title: Makkhaligosālavādavaṇṇanā
claim: the nāga-realm (nāgabhavana) is [reckoned as] a nāga-circle/domain, like the Mahiṃsaka domain

title: 7. Mahāsakuludāyisuttavaṇṇanā
claim: A nāga's innate power (nāgiddhi) lets it assume a form of another species, distinguished from iddhi-made forms.

title: 5. Cūḷakammavibhaṅgasuttavaṇṇanā
claim: Beings such as Devadatta are reborn as nāgas, where unwholesome kamma yields its result.

title: 9. Supaṇṇasaṃyuttavaṇṇanā
claim: Nāgas are sevenfold by clan/birth (Kambala, Assatara, Dhataraṭṭha, etc.); some are so powerful that supaṇṇas cannot carry them off; they dwell between the seven encircling mountains.

title: 3. Nāgasuttavaṇṇanā
claim: Nāgas gain strength and enlarge their bodies at the confluence of the great rivers and the ocean.

title: 4. Nidānasuttavaṇṇanā
claim: Beings such as Devadatta are reborn as nāgas, where unwholesome kamma yields its result.

title: 8-9. Devāsurasaṅgāmasuttādivaṇṇanā
claim: Nāgas (denoted by 'uraga') are strong in water, and are stationed as guards on Sineru's first terrace.

title: Tatiyasaṅgītikathāvaṇṇanā
claim: The Kāḷa nāga-king, having seen the living Buddha, magically manifested a likeness of his form.

title: Ācariyaparamparakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: A nāga-king, addressed as lord of the uragas/nāgas, is portrayed as powerful enough to hurl the whole earth, yet unable to terrify the sage.

title: Ācariyaparamparakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: A nāga(-king) receives Dhamma instruction (nāgānusāsana) from the elder and is established in wholesome states.

title: Ācariyaparamparakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: At Nāgadīpa the Buddha pacifies the warring nāga-kings Cūḷodara and Mahodara and 80 crores of nāgas, teaching them Dhamma so they rejoice and are reconciled.

title: Ācariyaparamparakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: The nāga-king Maṇiakkhika invites the Buddha, who teaches Dhamma to him and his retinue of nāga-maidens.

title: Ācariyaparamparakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: Sea-dwelling nāgas, seeking the Bodhi-tree, conjure storms and darkness (vikubbana), and are driven off by Saṅghamittā in garuḷa-form.

title: Ācariyaparamparakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: Back-reference to the Buddha's taming/conversion of the nāgas at Nāgadīpa (nāgadamana).

title: Verañjakaṇḍavaṇṇanā
claim: Nagas (glossed by uraga) are mighty in water and guard Mount Sineru's first terrace.

title: Verañjakaṇḍavaṇṇanā
claim: Nagas wage a bloodless war with the asuras at Sineru, driving them back; if outmatched they ally with the supannas.

title: Verañjakaṇḍavaṇṇanā
claim: A verse asks by what conduct/merit this naga's great celestial mansion arose.

title: Dūtakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: A naga (or supanna) is a tiracchanagata and dies in its own serpent-form, so the offence is pacittiya not thullaccaya.

title: Mucalindakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: The naga-king Mucalinda's body-heat and coils (bhoga) shelter the Buddha.

title: Sunidhavassakāravatthukathāvaṇṇanā
claim: Vatthu-vijja experts identify underground spots as the dwelling-claims of nagas (and yakkhas, bhutas).

title: Mahāsamudde aṭṭhacchariyakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: Nagas include those dwelling on the wave-crests and those in [celestial] mansions.

title: 22. Pabbajjāvinicchayakathā
claim: A naga (or supanna etc.), being a non-human animal (tiracchanagata), may not be ordained/given upasampada.

title: Tiracchānagatavatthukathāvaṇṇanā
claim: The monk-naga reverts to its naga-existence (nagayoni) when mating with its own kind or sleeping; cannot hold human form.

title: Uruvelapāṭihāriyakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: The subdued [fire-]naga, its power crushed, receives refuges and precepts and attends the Buddha all night.

title: Tiracchānavatthukathāvaṇṇanā
claim: The naga is water-dwelling and frog-eating; its serpent-body manifests (e.g. when asleep).

title: Ācariyaparamparakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: Dawn-coloured clay originates from the naga-realm (nagabhavana) and is brought by nagas.

title: 22. Pabbajjāvinicchayakathā
claim: The Buddha tells the naga: you are of non-growing nature (aviruddhi-dhamma) in this Dhamma-Vinaya - incapable of path-growth.

title: Nidānavaṇṇanā
claim: A naga (naga-manavaka), a non-human animal (tiracchanagata), is one whose upasampada is prohibited.

title: Pabbajjākathā
claim: A naga (or supanna, yakkha, even Sakka), called tiracchanagata, may not be ordained.

title: Mahāvibhaṅgasaṅgahakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: Tiracchanagata is glossed as a naga or supanna - classing the naga as a (non-human) animal, for the pit-digging rule.

title: Paṭhamapārājikakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: Nagas are reckoned among footless beings (apada), in the snake (ahi) category of the sattanikaya.

title: Dutiyapārājikakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: Nagas (and garulas), with their youths and maidens, may sit running a shop in human guise.

title: Tatiyapārājikakathāvaṇṇanā
claim: Nagas possess naga-iddhi: venom by sight, bite, and touch, killing thereby.

title: 3. Mucalindakathā
claim: The naga-king Mucalinda's coils (bhoga), seven-fold, encircle and shelter the Buddha.

title: 49. Tiracchānagatavatthu
claim: The naga, despite deva-like fortune, loathes its naga-existence (nagayoni, one of the four naga birth-modes).

title: 49. Tiracchānagatavatthu
claim: The naga eats water-dwelling frogs and mates with a same-species nagini, then loathes its state.

title: 49. Tiracchānagatavatthu
claim: Examines the naga's status as tiracchanagata (apaya-included, ahetuka-rebirth).

title: 1. Surāpānasikkhāpada-atthayojanā
claim: A monk overpowers the nāga's fiery majesty (teja/ānubhāva) by his own power
